title: Proteomics Perturbation Atlas metadata ontology
description: Draft application ontology for multi-level PPA metadata extracted from treatment, sample/source, and MS workflow prompts, with extensions for experimental design, workflow selection, and evidence-aware curation.

treatment.description: Primary biological, clinical, environmental, and exposure variables plus treatment attributes.

sample_source.description: Organism, cell type, cell line, tissue, biofluid, sample scope, and provenance metadata.

assay.description: Acquisition, labeling, quantification, proteomics mode, gas-phase separation, enrichment, and specialized workflow metadata.

experimental_design.description: Control/treatment structure, timepoints, replicates, multiplexing, and contrast metadata.

processing.description: Workflow selection, search, quantification, differential outputs, sample labeling, and batch correction metadata.

curation.description: Evidence, confidence, field provenance, mapping status, and review state.

relations.definition: Class-subclass relation connecting a child ontology term to its parent term.
Mereological relation for terms that are components of a broader structure or workflow.
Relation linking a source, sample, or cell line to its derivation context.
Cross-reference relation to an upstream ontology or controlled vocabulary term.
Project, sample, or contrast has a perturbing agent.
Treatment has dose, concentration, intensity, or MOI attribute.
Treatment has duration or exposure time attribute.
Sample or contrast has an associated timepoint.
Contrast has a control or reference arm.
Contrast has a case, treatment, or experimental arm.
Sample or project has a biological source entity.
Sample or project has an explicitly stated cell type.
Sample or project has an explicitly stated cell line.
Sample or project has tissue, organ, fluid, specimen, or cohort source.
Sample or project has material scope.
Project or run has MS acquisition type.
Project, run, or sample has labeling strategy.
Project or output has quantification strategy.
Project has proteomics mode.
Project has specialized workflow or modifier.
Stage 4 analysis has selected base workflow.
Stage 4 analysis has selected modifier workflow.
Analysis output has differential output type.
Sample or run has batch identifier.
Annotation has supporting evidence text or provenance.
Annotation has mapping or review status.

nodes.definition: Application ontology for harmonizing perturbation, biological source, assay, study design, and reanalysis metadata for the Proteomics Perturbation Atlas.
Metadata describing the primary biological, cohort, environmental, or exposure-related variables compared in a proteomics project.
Metadata describing the biological material, organism context, cell identity, tissue/source, and scope of material measured by proteomics.
Metadata describing the mass spectrometry acquisition, labeling, quantification, proteomics mode, enrichment, and specialized workflow characteristics.
Metadata describing how samples, cohorts, channels, timepoints, replicates, and contrasts are structured.
Metadata describing automated workflow selection, search, quantification, aggregation, differential outputs, sample labeling, and batch correction state.
Metadata describing evidence, confidence, mapping quality, and abstention or conflict states for extracted ontology annotations.
A primary biological, cohort-level, or exposure-related variable that differentiates experimental groups and is central to biological interpretation.
Exposure to an exogenous small molecule, inhibitor, activator, toxicant, therapeutic compound, degrader, or vehicle that differentiates experimental groups.
Addition of an extracellular signaling molecule such as a cytokine, growth factor, hormone, or receptor ligand to activate a biological state.
current_field_mapping: treatment_category: Growth Factor or Cytokine Stimulation

nodes.definition: Direct alteration of gene function, sequence, expression, dosage, or genetic background defining the biological comparison.
Change in nutrient availability, metabolite exposure, oxygen availability, carbon source, or metabolic flux state that defines the comparison.
Perturbation that changes reactive oxygen or nitrogen species, antioxidant systems, thiol state, or broader redox balance.
Change in extracellular or intracellular acidity, alkalinity, salt, ion, or osmotic state that defines the comparison.
current_field_mapping: treatment_category: pH or Ionic Strength Changes

nodes.definition: Application of force, geometry, substrate, adhesion, stiffness, deformation, or other non-radiation physical stimulus that defines the comparison.
Exposure to ionizing radiation, ultraviolet light, visible-light activation, photodynamic treatment, or related light-induced stress.
Exposure of a host biological system to a virus, bacterium, fungus, parasite, or pathogen-derived infection model that defines the comparison.
Disease status, disease subtype, disease stage, pathology state, treatment response, or clinical outcome state defining the comparison.
Comparison where anatomical region, organ subregion, spatial niche, or microenvironment defines the biological groups.
Comparison where activation, differentiation, cell cycle, senescence, death, transition, coculture, or interaction state defines the biological groups.
Comparison where a protein, biomolecule, PTM, conformational, thermal, complex, or molecular input state is the primary biological variable rather than only an assay readout.
Non-baseline temperature exposure defining the comparison.
Chemical treatment using a compound that inhibits enzyme, receptor, channel, transporter, pathway, or complex function.
Treatment using a compound that inhibits protein kinase activity or kinase signaling.
Treatment using a compound that inhibits proteasomal degradation or proteasome catalytic activity.
Treatment using a compound targeting chromatin, histone, DNA methylation, or epigenetic reader/writer/eraser machinery.
Chemical treatment that inhibits a metabolic enzyme, transporter, or metabolic pathway.
Chemical treatment using a small molecule that activates a receptor, enzyme, pathway, or signaling process.
Chemical treatment using a degrader, molecular glue, or PROTAC to reduce target protein abundance.
Chemical exposure to toxicants, poisons, or cytotoxic agents used as a primary biological comparison.
Chemical treatment that induces DNA damage or replication stress.
Chemical treatment that perturbs endoplasmic reticulum proteostasis or unfolded protein response state.
Chemical treatment that perturbs protein folding, degradation, chaperone function, or proteome stability.
Control condition exposed to solvent or carrier without active treatment agent.
Exposure to two or more chemical or drug agents as a combined perturbation.
Chemical or drug treatment explicitly varied across concentration or dose levels.
Stimulation using cytokines or immune signaling proteins.
Stimulation using an interleukin ligand.
Stimulation using type I, type II, or type III interferons.
Stimulation using TNF or TNF-superfamily ligands.
Stimulation using chemokine ligands.
Stimulation using growth factors that activate growth, survival, motility, or differentiation pathways.
Stimulation using epidermal growth factor family ligands.
Stimulation using TGF-beta superfamily ligands.
Stimulation using insulin or insulin-like growth factor signaling ligands.
Stimulation using endocrine or paracrine hormones.
Stimulation by ligands or agonists of immune receptors.
Genetic perturbation involving complete or partial loss of a gene or locus.
Knockout or gene disruption generated using CRISPR-based genome editing.
Reduction of gene expression by RNA interference, antisense, shRNA, siRNA, CRISPRi, or related approaches.
Increased expression of a gene, protein, mutant, construct, or transgene that defines the comparison.
Comparison between wild-type and mutant or variant alleles, proteins, or genotypes.
Genome editing event or engineered genotype that defines the biological comparison.
Genetic perturbation controlled by inducible, conditional, or tissue-specific systems.
Comparison involving engineered transgenic models or lines.
Comparison involving restoration or rescue of a genetic defect.
Reduced or removed nutrient, serum, amino acid, glucose, lipid, or growth support condition.
Removal or reduction of serum from growth medium as the primary comparison.
Removal or limitation of one or more amino acids.
Removal or limitation of glucose availability.
Addition or restoration of nutrients, metabolites, or cofactors.

nodes.preferred_label: altered carbon source or metabolic flux
nodes.definition: Comparison defined by different carbon sources or altered metabolic flux state.
Altered oxygen state such as hypoxia, normoxia, anoxia, or reoxygenation.
Comparison among metabolic programs, energetic states, or metabolic phenotypes not captured by a single nutrient.
Exposure or condition increasing reactive oxygen species or oxidative damage.
Condition that alters nitric oxide or reactive nitrogen species signaling or damage.
Treatment with antioxidant compounds or enhanced antioxidant state.
Perturbation that changes cysteine, thiol, disulfide, or redox-sensitive protein state.
Perturbation connecting redox state with protein folding, proteostasis, or stress response.
Change in pH that defines the primary comparison.
Change in sodium chloride or other salt concentration that defines the comparison.
Change in osmotic state using salt, sorbitol, mannitol, or osmotic agents.
Change in calcium, magnesium, zinc, iron, or other ion availability or signaling.
Use of an ionophore or ion transport modulator to define the comparison.
Mechanical perturbation by fluid shear or flow.
Mechanical stretching, cyclic strain, or deformation of cells or tissues.
Mechanical compression or pressure applied to the biological system.
Comparison defined by matrix, gel, or substrate stiffness.
Comparison defined by patterned substrates, confinement, geometry, topography, or spatial constraints.
Physical stimulus using electric or magnetic field exposure.
Exposure to gamma rays, X-rays, particles, or other ionizing radiation.
Exposure to ultraviolet light as stress or signaling stimulus.
Visible light exposure used for optogenetic, photoreceptor, or light-induced signaling experiments.
Light activation of photosensitizers or photodynamic stress protocols.
Infection or exposure to a virus, viral particle, viral vector, or viral replication model.
Infection or exposure to bacteria or bacterial challenge that defines the comparison.
Infection or exposure to a fungal organism that defines the comparison.
Infection or exposure to a parasite that defines the comparison.
Control exposure that mimics infection procedure without active pathogen.
Exposure to microbial components such as LPS or poly(I:C) when modeled as pathogen-derived stimulation rather than infection.
Comparison between diseased and healthy or unaffected controls.
Comparison between tumor tissue and matched or unmatched non-tumor tissue.
Comparison among cancer types, subtypes, molecular subtypes, or histologies.
Comparison among disease severity, stage, grade, or progression categories.
Comparison between primary lesion and metastatic disease.
Clinical comparison between responders, nonresponders, resistant, relapsed, sensitive, or remission states.
Comparison defined by clinical outcome, prognosis, survival, remodeling, or recovery status.
Comparison based on disease-linked genotype, risk allele, or mutation carrier status in a clinical cohort.
Comparison based on disease duration, acute flare, chronic state, or remission.
Comparison among regions, zones, lobes, layers, or anatomical substructures within an organ.
Comparison among niches or microenvironments such as tumor core, invasive front, stroma, or perivascular areas.
Comparison among spatially resolved cellular compartments when the spatial state is the biological variable.
Comparison among anatomical developmental stages or morphogenetic regions.
Comparison between activated, stimulated, resting, quiescent, or unstimulated cell states when not captured by a specific ligand branch.
Comparison among progenitor, differentiated, induced, lineage-committed, or maturation states.
Comparison among cell cycle phases, synchronized states, proliferating, or arrested cells.
Comparison involving senescent versus proliferating, young, control, or non-senescent states.
Comparison involving apoptosis, necroptosis, ferroptosis, pyroptosis, or survival/death states.
Comparison involving EMT, MET, or transition/intermediate epithelial-mesenchymal states.
Comparison based on co-culture, cell-cell interaction, conditioned medium, stromal interaction, or immune interaction state.
Comparison among organoid, spheroid, induced differentiation, or tissue model states.
Comparison where modified versus unmodified, phosphorylated, glycosylated, acetylated, ubiquitinated, or other PTM state is the biological grouping.
Comparison where protein conformation, folding state, complex conformation, or structural state is the biological variable.
Comparison based on protein thermal stability or melting behavior as the biological variable.
Comparison based on complex assembly, disassembly, binding, stoichiometry, or interaction state.
Comparison between modified and unmodified input molecules, ligands, substrates, biomaterials, or experimental inputs.
Exposure to temperature above baseline physiological or growth condition.
Exposure to temperature below baseline physiological or growth condition.
Temperature series, gradient, ramp, or shift used as a perturbation rather than only analytical TPP.
Temperature elevation matching fever or mild hyperthermia state.
An attribute that qualifies a treatment comparison, including agent, dose, duration, route, schedule, or combination structure.
Named compound, ligand, pathogen, gene, environmental stimulus, or other entity that mediates the perturbation.
Dose, concentration, amount, multiplicity of infection, radiation dose, or exposure intensity.
Length of time for which a perturbation is applied before sampling.
Medium, route, delivery method, infection route, culture condition, or exposure context.
Order, repeated dosing, pulse, chase, washout, pretreatment, or recovery schedule.
Attribute capturing multiple simultaneous or sequential perturbing agents.
Attribute defining vehicle, untreated, mock, wild-type, healthy, baseline, or reference control state.
The biological source from which proteomics measurements were derived, including organism, cell, tissue, fluid, or specimen context.
Species, taxonomic, or mixed-species context of the biological source.
Annotation that the proteomics source explicitly includes human cells, tissues, biofluids, participants, or human-derived cell lines.
Annotation that the proteomics source explicitly appears to be non-human only.
Annotation that organism or source is not clearly specified.
Biological source or experimental context containing human plus non-human cells, tissues, xenograft, microbiome, spike-in, pathogen, or host-pathogen material.
NCBI taxonomy identifier or organism name used to define project selection or biological source.
Non-human organism used as a model or source of proteomics material.
Cell-based biological material including primary cells, established cell lines, derivatives, or cell-type aggregation categories.
Most specific explicitly stated human cell type or primary cell identity used for proteomics.
Higher-level controlled rollup category for aggregating exact human cell types in atlas integration.
Most specific explicitly stated named human cell line, derivative, subline, clone, engineered line, or established culture model.
Higher-level rollup grouping related cell lines, derivatives, clones, or formatting variants under a stable parent family.
State or derivation information describing induced, differentiated, immortalized, transformed, primary, organoid, xenograft, or engineered source context.
Aggregation category for T cells, B cells, NK cells, plasma cells, and other lymphoid cells.
Aggregation category for monocytes, macrophages, dendritic cells, neutrophils, microglia, and related myeloid cells.
Aggregation category for PBMCs, peripheral blood mononuclear cells, whole blood immune fractions, or generic immune cells.
Aggregation category for embryonic, induced pluripotent, hematopoietic, neural, mesenchymal, or other progenitor cells.
Aggregation category for epithelial cells and epithelial-derived primary cell identities.
Aggregation category for endothelial and vascular cell identities.
Aggregation category for fibroblasts, stromal cells, mesenchymal cells, and related support cells.
Aggregation category for neurons, astrocytes, oligodendrocytes, glial cells, and neural cells.
Aggregation category for cardiomyocytes, skeletal muscle, smooth muscle, and contractile cells.
Aggregation category for hepatocytes, adipocytes, and metabolic parenchymal cells.
Aggregation category for specialized human cell identities that do not fit other major rollups.
Aggregation category for primary tumor cell-type labels not expressed as named cell lines.
Cell line family rollup for HEK293 and explicitly stated derivatives or formatting variants.
Cell line family rollup for HeLa and explicitly stated derivatives or formatting variants.
Cell line family rollup for A549 and explicitly stated derivatives or formatting variants.
Cell line family rollup for MCF-7 and explicitly stated derivatives or formatting variants.
Cell line family rollup for PC3 and explicitly stated derivatives or formatting variants.
Cell line family rollup for THP-1 and explicitly stated derivatives or formatting variants.
Cell line family rollup for U87MG and explicitly stated derivatives or formatting variants.
Cell line family rollup for U251MG and explicitly stated derivatives or formatting variants.
Cell line family rollup for A375 and explicitly stated derivatives or formatting variants.
Cell line family rollup for Jurkat and explicitly stated derivatives or formatting variants.
Cell line family rollup for K562 and explicitly stated derivatives or formatting variants.
Cell line family rollup for U2OS and explicitly stated derivatives or formatting variants.
Cell line family rollup for VCaP and explicitly stated derivatives or formatting variants.
Cell line family rollup for HepG2 and explicitly stated derivatives or formatting variants.
Cell line family rollup for HCT116 and explicitly stated derivatives or formatting variants.
Cell line family rollup for RPE1 and explicitly stated derivatives or formatting variants.
Cell line family rollup for BJ fibroblast and explicitly stated derivatives or formatting variants.
Cell line family rollup for SH-SY5Y and explicitly stated derivatives or formatting variants.
Source material explicitly described as primary cells or directly obtained from donor material before establishment as a line.
Source material described as immortalized but not necessarily tumor-derived.

nodes.preferred_label: transformed or cancer cell line source
nodes.definition: Source material described as tumor-derived, transformed, or cancer cell line.
Source material described as differentiated from progenitors, stem cells, or induced cells.
Three-dimensional organoid, spheroid, tumoroid, or organotypic culture source.
Source material derived from xenograft, patient-derived xenograft, or human material grown in non-human host.
Source material derived from mixed cell coculture, interaction model, or conditioned interaction system.
Source material described as engineered, edited, transfected, knock-in, knockout, or expressing a construct.
Human tissue, organ, biofluid, specimen, cohort descriptor, or source term that is not itself a cell type or cell line.
Named human tissue or tissue bulk source.
Named organ, organ part, anatomical structure, or anatomical region source.
Human body fluid, secretion, or circulating fluid used as proteomics material.
Blood plasma used as proteomics material.
Blood serum used as proteomics material.
Cerebrospinal fluid used as proteomics material.
Urine used as proteomics material.
Saliva used as proteomics material.
Tumor, lesion, diseased tissue region, biopsy, or pathological specimen source.
Participant cohort descriptor such as patients, donors, healthy individuals, responders, or clinical subgroup.
Extracellular vesicles, exosomes, microvesicles, or vesicle-enriched biofluid material.
Breadth or subset of proteomics material analyzed relative to the original biological source.
Bulk cellular proteome, total lysate, whole-cell extract, or comparable unfractionated cellular material.
Specific subset of the proteome defined by compartment, enrichment, secretion, surface, PTM, organelle, or biochemical fraction.
Proteomics performed on purified protein, isolated complex, affinity-purified material, recombinant protein, ribosome, proteasome, or similar purified entity.
MHC-bound peptide repertoire or immunopeptidomics material.
Secreted proteins, conditioned medium, extracellular proteins, or secretome-enriched material.
Extracellular vesicle or exosome proteome.
Cell surface, plasma membrane exposed, or surface-captured proteome.
Organelle-enriched or organelle-specific proteomics material.
Affinity-purified protein complex or interaction-enriched material.
Explicit compartment-specific, organelle-specific, or fraction-enriched cellular material.
Nuclear or nucleoplasm-enriched proteomics material.
Chromatin-bound, DNA-associated, or chromosome-enriched material.
Membrane, plasma membrane, or surface-enriched material.
Mitochondria-enriched proteomics material.
Cytosol-enriched proteomics material.
Ribosome, polysome, translation complex, or ribosome-associated material.
Proteasome, ubiquitin-proteasome system, or degradation complex-enriched material.
Traceability and mapping attributes connecting source, sample, file, evidence span, and repository records.
BioSamples or related sample accession linked to the proteomics sample.
ProteomeXchange/PRIDE project accession such as PXD identifier.
Mapping between raw data file, mzML, sample name, replicate, and biological condition.
Verbatim manuscript, PRIDE, or SDRF text supporting a source annotation.
Sample name, run name, raw file stem, or label used in repository metadata or filenames.
PRIDE, SDRF, PMC, or other repository-derived metadata source used to support annotations.
Primary mass spectrometry acquisition strategy used at project or major workflow level.
MS/MS acquisition where precursor ions are selected for fragmentation based on intensity or predefined decision rules.
MS/MS acquisition where predefined m/z windows or broad precursor populations are fragmented independent of individual precursor selection.
Targeted MS acquisition such as PRM, SRM, or MRM focused on predefined precursors, transitions, or peptides.
Acquisition targeting intact proteins or proteoforms rather than digested peptides.
Acquisition using all-ion fragmentation rather than precursor-specific isolation or standard windowed DIA.
No explicit or sufficiently specific evidence supports a known acquisition type.
Subtype or implementation of data-independent acquisition.
DIA interpreted using a spectral library or assay library.
DIA interpreted without a project-specific or empirical spectral library.
DIA combined with trapped ion mobility and parallel accumulation serial fragmentation.
DIA using scanning or sliding SWATH isolation windows.
DIA design using gas-phase fractionation or segmented acquisition to increase coverage.
DIA detected but no subtype is clearly stated.
Project-level chemical, isotopic, metabolic, isobaric, or label-free quantification label design.
Quantification without isotope or isobaric labeling, typically using MS1 intensity or DIA fragment areas.
Isobaric labeling using tandem mass tags, including TMTpro and multiplexed TMT designs.
Isobaric labeling using iTRAQ reagents.
Metabolic stable isotope labeling by amino acids in cell culture.
synonyms: stable isotope labeling by amino acids in cell culture

nodes.definition: SILAC design using a heavy labeled reference or mixture for comparison to unlabeled samples.
Pulse SILAC labeling used for protein synthesis or turnover measurements.
SILAC-based time-dependent labeling for turnover or kinetic analysis.
Chemical isotope labeling by reductive dimethylation.
Stable isotope labeling using uniform or residue-specific nitrogen-15 incorporation.
Stable isotope labeling using oxygen-18 incorporation, typically at peptide C-termini.
Absolute quantification using stable isotope-labeled reference peptides.
Explicit labeling strategy not covered by LFQ, TMT, iTRAQ, SILAC, or dimethyl.
No explicit or sufficiently specific evidence supports a known labeling strategy.
Detailed isotope labeling channel, residue, site, mass shift, isotope composition, and plex metadata.
Reference or light channel with zero mass shift for specified residues or sites.
Intermediate isotope channel in triplex or multi-channel isotope labeling.
Heavy isotope channel with positive mass shift for specified residues or sites.
Stable isotope mass shift assigned to lysine residues.
Stable isotope mass shift assigned to arginine residues.
Stable isotope or chemical isotope mass shift assigned to peptide or protein N-termini.
Stable isotope mass shift assigned to peptide C-termini, including 18O labeling.
Number of isotope channels in an isotope labeling design.
Signal basis used to quantify peptide, protein, site, or feature abundance.
Quantification based on precursor MS1 ion intensity, extracted ion chromatograms, or feature intensities.
Quantification based on isobaric reporter ions measured in MS2 spectra.
Quantification based on isobaric reporter ions measured in MS3 or SPS-MS3 spectra.
Quantification based on fragment-ion chromatogram areas from DIA data.
Quantification based on integrated targeted PRM/SRM/MRM peak areas.
Quantification based on spectrum counts, PSM counts, NSAF, or related counting measures.
No explicit evidence supports a known quantification strategy.
Overall analytical proteomics mode or peptide/protein target class.
Proteomics mode based on enzymatically digested peptides used to infer proteins.
Proteomics mode analyzing intact proteins or proteoforms.
Proteomics mode analyzing large peptides or protein fragments between bottom-up and top-down scale.
Analysis of endogenous peptides without conventional proteolytic digestion.
Analysis of MHC-bound or antigen-presented peptides.
Proteomics mode enriching modified peptides or proteins such as phospho-, glyco-, acetyl-, or ubiquitin-modified molecules.
No explicit evidence supports a known proteomics mode.
Ion mobility or FAIMS-based gas-phase separation used in MS acquisition.
Field asymmetric waveform ion mobility spectrometry separation before mass analysis.
Trapped ion mobility separation used in acquisition.
Traveling-wave ion mobility separation used in acquisition.
Explicit statement that gas-phase separation was not used.
No explicit evidence about gas-phase separation.
Specialized proteomics or structural workflow that changes reanalysis compatibility or requires workflow modifiers.
Cross-linking mass spectrometry workflow measuring chemically crosslinked peptide pairs or residue restraints.
Limited proteolysis mass spectrometry workflow reporting protein structural accessibility or conformational change.
Hydrogen-deuterium exchange mass spectrometry workflow measuring exchange behavior or structural dynamics.
Native mass spectrometry workflow analyzing intact complexes or non-denatured molecular assemblies.
Workflow enriching or specifically analyzing crosslinked peptides.
Workflow based on proximity labeling enzymes or chemical labeling of nearby proteins.
Workflow using temperature-dependent solubility or stability changes, including TPP or CETSA-MS.
Affinity purification or interactome workflow analyzing enriched protein complexes or binding partners.
Workflow profiling protein complexes by fractionation or complexome analysis.
Proteomics workflow on single cells or very low input single-cell samples.
Workflow that preserves or analyzes spatial proteome organization across compartments, regions, or spatial locations.
Proteomics workflow analyzing microbial communities or mixed taxonomic proteomes.
Workflow specifically measuring redox state or cysteine reactivity.
Molecular target class, enrichment chemistry, or assay subset enriched before MS analysis.
Enrichment of phosphorylated peptides or proteins.
Enrichment of glycosylated peptides, proteins, or glycans.
Enrichment of ubiquitylated peptides, diGly remnants, or ubiquitin-modified proteins.
Enrichment of acetylated peptides or proteins.
Enrichment or specific analysis of protein or peptide N-termini.
ICAT or related cysteine-focused enrichment and labeling workflow.
Enrichment of MHC-bound peptides or antigen presentation peptides.
Enrichment of cell surface proteins or peptides.
Enrichment or collection of secreted proteins or conditioned medium.
Enrichment of organelle-specific material before proteomics analysis.
Enrichment of SUMOylated peptides or proteins, including QQTGG remnant signatures such as K-QQTGG.
Instrument, analyzer, fragmentation, scan, isolation, raw-file, and mzML-derived run metadata used for evidence and workflow selection.
Mass spectrometer instrument model or platform name.
Manufacturer or vendor of the instrument.
Fragmentation method such as HCD, CID, ETD, EThcD, UVPD, or related modes.
Mass analyzer or scan analyzer configuration.
Raw-file or mzML evidence supporting acquisition classification.
Isolation window width or m/z range used for precursor or DIA window isolation.
Original raw mass spectrometry file format.
Provenance of raw-to-mzML conversion, parser, tool, or conversion status.
How biological groups, control arms, treatment arms, cohorts, channels, and reference states are arranged.
Baseline, vehicle, mock, wild-type, untreated, healthy, or reference group used as comparison baseline.
Perturbed, exposed, mutant, diseased, infected, stimulated, treated, or case group compared to control.
Multiplexed channel or pooled reference sample used for normalization or cross-batch linking.
Design in which samples are naturally or experimentally paired, such as before-after or matched tumor-normal.
Design with two or more independent factors or crossed perturbations.
Clinical or cohort design comparing cases to controls.
Design with multiple dose, concentration, MOI, or exposure intensity levels.
Design comparing three or more biological or perturbation groups.
Timepoint structure or sampling schedule relative to baseline, treatment, infection, pulse, chase, recovery, or other event.
Timepoint before perturbation or at baseline.
Timepoint after perturbation or exposure.
Three or more ordered timepoints or explicitly temporal experimental series.
Timepoint in a pulse-chase or labeling-chase design.
Timepoint after treatment removal, recovery, or washout.
Timepoint sampling across circadian, oscillatory, or periodic biological cycles.
Timepoint across development, differentiation, maturation, or induction series.
Structure of biological replicates, technical replicates, fractions, channels, and files used to represent samples.
Independent biological replicate sample or donor replicate.
Repeated technical preparation, injection, or measurement of the same biological material.
Fractionated sample or LC/high-pH/gel/SEC fraction linked to a source sample.
TMT, iTRAQ, SILAC, dimethyl, or other multiplex channel representing a sample or reference.
Raw file corresponding to a sample replicate, injection, or fraction.
Pattern of observed and missing protein or peptide measurements across samples.
Type of differential comparison generated or implied by sample labels and design metadata.
Two-group comparison such as treatment versus control.
Comparison among more than two groups or conditions.
Differential model or contrast across dose or concentration levels.
Differential model based on time-dependent synthesis, degradation, turnover, or kinetic behavior.
Differential model incorporating PTM site intensity and occupancy state.
Differential comparison of clinical case and control cohorts.
Contrast that respects matched or paired sample design.
Automated assignment of base and modifier workflows that determine search, quantification, aggregation, and differential templates.
Primary workflow family selected from supported Stage 4 base workflows.
Base workflow for DDA label-free bottom-up proteomics.
Base workflow for DDA SILAC or metabolic isotope-label bottom-up proteomics.
Base workflow for DDA isobaric TMT or iTRAQ proteomics.
Base workflow for DDA dimethyl-labeled proteomics.
Base workflow for DIA label-free proteomics.
Compatible workflow modifier selected in addition to a base workflow to represent enrichment, specialized assay, or reanalysis-specific behavior.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing acetyl behavior or analysis requirements.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing AP-MS behavior or analysis requirements.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing complexome behavior or analysis requirements.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing dynamic SILAC behavior or analysis requirements.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing glyco behavior or analysis requirements.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing ICAT behavior or analysis requirements.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing immunopeptidomics behavior or analysis requirements.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing LiP-MS behavior or analysis requirements.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing metaproteomics behavior or analysis requirements.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing N-terminomics behavior or analysis requirements.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing phospho behavior or analysis requirements.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing proximity labeling behavior or analysis requirements.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing pSILAC behavior or analysis requirements.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing redox behavior or analysis requirements.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing SILAC-on-isobaric behavior or analysis requirements.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing single-cell behavior or analysis requirements.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing spatial behavior or analysis requirements.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing TPP behavior or analysis requirements.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing ubiquitin behavior or analysis requirements.
Stage 4 modifier workflow representing SUMOylome behavior or QQTGG-remnant-specific analysis requirements.
Numerical confidence score or decision status for workflow matching.
Fallback workflow generation when registry matching is low-confidence or incomplete.
Evidence terms, sample labels, or raw-file naming cues used to trigger Stage 4 modifier selection.
Protein database search configuration and parameterization used for peptide-spectrum matching and protein discovery.
Software used to search spectra against a sequence database.
Proteolytic enzyme or digestion specificity used for search.
Static modification applied to all matching residues or sites in search parameters.
Variable modification considered during database search.
FASTA or protein sequence database used for search.
Precursor mass tolerance parameter.
Fragment ion mass tolerance parameter.
FDR threshold used for PSM, peptide, protein, or site-level filtering.
Method or software logic used to infer proteins from peptides.
Output artifacts produced by search, quantification, aggregation, and normalization.
Per-file peptide-spectrum match table produced after search and processing.
Per-file peptide-level output table.
Per-file protein-level output table.
Cross-file matrix of protein, peptide, site, or feature abundance values.
Binary or structured representation of observed and missing values across the abundance matrix.
Normalization, transformation, or scaling applied to abundance values.
Summary of cross-file aggregation and dataset-level output state.
Run-level summary artifact describing workflow, outputs, failures, and metadata.
Type of differential output generated from abundance, site, isotope, enrichment, or specialized workflow matrices.
Protein-level differential output based on log2 fold change between conditions.
PTM or site-level differential output based on log2 fold change.
Site-level differential output incorporating site occupancy or occupancy correction.
Differential output based on isotope ratios such as SILAC or dimethyl ratios.
Differential output for synthesis, degradation, pulse-chase, or turnover kinetics.
Differential output based on fitted curves such as thermal shift, dose-response, or structural response curves.
Differential output based on percent oxidation, reduced fraction, or redox occupancy.
Limited proteolysis differential output corrected for abundance or accessibility effects.
Differential output based on enrichment score or pull-down/enrichment-specific scoring.
Operational labels and latent correction state used for control/treatment mapping and cross-PXD integration.
UMAP/DBSCAN or other clustering assignment of samples based on abundance profiles.
Sample condition label adjusted or validated using LLM interpretation of filenames, clusters, and publication context.
Operational label marking control, treatment, case, vehicle, mock, or other comparison group.
Project, PXD, run, acquisition batch, lab, instrument, or technical batch identifier.
Technical state used as reference or target for batch correction.
Abundance matrix decoded or transformed after batch correction.
Latent representation intended to preserve biological state during batch correction.
Latent representation intended to isolate technical or project-specific batch state.
Latent representation of sample-specific variation not assigned to known biology or batch state.
Operational assessment of whether a project can be processed by standard bottom-up reanalysis or requires specialized handling.
DDA or DIA bottom-up proteomics can be reanalyzed with standard PPA bottom-up workflows.
Project requires specialized workflow, modifier, or non-standard processing beyond standard bottom-up proteomics.

nodes.preferred_label: not suitable for standard bottom-up reanalysis
nodes.definition: Project is top-down, native MS, non-proteomics MS, or otherwise unsuitable for standard bottom-up reanalysis.
Compatibility cannot be determined from available evidence.
Evidence and provenance terms attached to extracted metadata annotations.
Short exact text span copied from INPUT_TEXT that directly supports the annotation.
Concise explanation connecting evidence to ontology mapping.
Numerical score representing extraction or mapping confidence.
Prompt or tool that produced or supported the annotation.
Provenance for a specific field value or ontology assertion.
Mapping from a PPA node to an upstream ontology, controlled vocabulary, or database term.
Named entity or identifier extracted as an example treatment agent, gene, disease, cell line, or source.
Explanation for returning null, Unknown, not applicable, or no mapping.
State of the ontology mapping after extraction, normalization, validation, and curation.
Annotation has sufficient evidence and maps to a stable ontology node.
Annotation is useful but lower-confidence, newly introduced, or awaiting expert review.
Clear biological comparison exists but no suitable current ontology node is available.
Available evidence supports conflicting annotations that require review.
Field is not applicable for this project, sample, or workflow.
Information is missing or insufficient for a confident mapping.
Candidate annotation was rejected because evidence was insufficient or violated guardrails.
Annotation requires manual expert curation before downstream use.
Ontology node retained for compatibility but no longer recommended.
